Calculation Report

Swing Set Digital Twin

This report explains how the digital twin in the dist build turns user inputs into live swing motion, chain tension, beam stress, deflection, a strength-to-stress ratio, and the telemetry graphs shown in the simulation.

Model Snapshot

Motion Damped pendulum solved at 60 Hz
Loads Gravity plus centripetal chain tension
Beam Simply supported center-load approximation
Output Stress, deflection, model ratio, live graphs

How The Simulation Runs

The app treats each swing as a pendulum. Every animation frame it accumulates real elapsed time, then advances the physics in fixed 1/60 second steps. The fixed time step keeps the calculation stable even if the browser frame rate varies.

1. Inputs Child mass, seat mass, chain length, start angle, beam span, beam section, wood material, swing count, and scenario.
2. Motion A damped pendulum equation updates angle and angular velocity.
3. Tension The current speed and angle are converted into chain tension.
4. Beam The vertical load component is used to estimate moment, stress, and deflection.
5. Display Results drive the 3D visualization, status panel, load colors, and scrolling plots.

Pendulum Motion

The angular state is the swing angle theta and angular velocity omega. The app uses gravity, chain length, and a small damping coefficient to model swing-down behavior.

theta'' = -(g / L) sin(theta) - c theta'
Symbol Meaning Value In App
g Gravity 9.81 m/s^2
L Chain length User input, default 1.9 m
c Damping coefficient 0.012
dt Physics time step 1/60 s

The equation is integrated with a fourth-order Runge-Kutta step. That means each update samples the slope four times and blends the result, giving smoother motion than a simple Euler update.

Load Calculation

After the pendulum angle and angular velocity are updated, the app computes seat speed, radial acceleration, and chain tension. The total swinging mass is the child mass plus the seat mass.

m = childMass + seatMass
speed = abs(omega * L)
centripetalTerm = m * L * omega^2
gravityAlongChain = m * g * cos(theta)
chainTension = (centripetalTerm + gravityAlongChain) * scenarioMultiplier

Tension is highest near the bottom of the swing because speed is highest and cos(theta) is close to 1. At the top of the arc, speed is lower and the gravity component along the chain is smaller, so tension drops.

Scenario Multipliers

The scenarios are not separate finite-element simulations. They are quick load cases that change starting angle, number of swings, phase relationship, and a load multiplier.

Scenario Start Angle Swing Count Load Multiplier
Static Child 0 deg 1 1.0
Normal Swing 30 deg 1 1.0
High Energy Swing 55 deg 1 1.5
Two Children, In Phase 40 deg 2 2.0
Two Children, Out Of Phase 40 deg 2 1.2

When the swing count is 2, the app also applies a distribution factor of 1.85 to the force components. This approximates two nearby dynamic loads without fully modeling the beam as a multi-load continuous system.

From Chain Tension To Beam Force

Chain tension is resolved into vertical and horizontal components using the current swing angle. The structural beam calculations use the vertical force component.

verticalForce = chainTension * cos(theta) * distributionFactor
horizontalForce = chainTension * sin(abs(theta)) * distributionFactor

The vertical component drives bending in the top beam. The horizontal component is still shown as useful telemetry because it represents the side load that the frame and bracing must react.

Beam Stress And Deflection

The top beam is approximated as a rectangular simply supported beam with a central point load. The app converts the beam width and height from millimeters to meters, computes section properties, then estimates bending moment, stress, and deflection.

sectionModulus = b * h^2 / 6
secondMoment = b * h^3 / 12
bendingMoment = verticalForce * beamLength / 4
bendingStressMPa = (bendingMoment / sectionModulus) / 1,000,000
deflectionMm = verticalForce * beamLength^3 / (48 * E * secondMoment) * 1,000

These are classic beam equations for a simply supported span with a center load: maximum moment is P L / 4 and center deflection is P L^3 / 48 E I.

Simplified Strength-to-Stress Ratio

The app compares calculated bending stress against the selected wood material's modulus of rupture. This ratio is a model output, not a safety factor or approval.

modelRatio = modulusOfRuptureMPa / bendingStressMPa
Model ratio Model label Interpretation
4.0 or higher HIGH MODEL RATIO Large reserve in the simplified bending check.
2.5 to 4.0 MODERATE MODEL RATIO Reasonable reserve for the model assumptions.
1.5 to 2.5 LOW MODEL RATIO Close enough that details, fasteners, knots, and joints matter a lot.
Below 1.5 NEAR MODEL LIMIT Stress reserve is low in the simplified model.

What The Report Does Not Claim

This digital twin is an explanatory design and visualization model. It is useful for understanding load trends, dynamic amplification, and why the bottom of the swing is the demanding case. It is not a stamped structural analysis.

No fastener check No joint slip model No local bearing check No wood defects No fatigue model No full frame FEA

Inputs And Constants

  • Default Geometry Chain length 1.9 m, beam length 3.0 m, beam section 140 mm by 140 mm.
  • Default Mass Child mass 30 kg, seat mass 3 kg, total simulated payload 33 kg.
  • Gravity 9.81 m/s^2.
  • Damping 0.012 in the pendulum equation.
Douglas Fir Modulus of Rupture 50 MPa
Douglas Fir E 13 GPa
Cedar Modulus of Rupture 37 MPa
Cedar E 7.7 GPa
Pressure Pine Modulus of Rupture 45 MPa
Pressure Pine E 11 GPa

Telemetry

The graph stores the latest 200 samples. It plots time, swing angle in degrees, chain tension in newtons, and beam stress in MPa. The displayed 3D color mode maps higher stress ratios toward warning colors.

Plain-English Summary

The simulation first asks, "Where is the child in the swing arc and how fast are they moving?" It then asks, "How much chain tension does that motion create?" Finally it asks, "If that load acts on the top beam, what bending stress and deflection result?"

Modulus of rupture is the bending strength value used for each wood option. The app divides this strength by the calculated beam stress to estimate the model ratio.
